text: The surgeon performs an intracorporeal esophageal jejunal stoma creation using a circular stapler, aligning the esophageal and jejunal ends for anastomosis.

The surgeon clamps the distal esophagus with a laparoscopic intestinal clamp. A laparoscopic purse string clamp, lap jack, is inserted through the twelve millimeter left middle trocar. A two-headed straight needle is used to create a purse string suture around the esophagus. The suture is pulled through the right upper trocar. The esophagus is transected below the lap jack using a hook or spatula-type bovie, and the lap jack is released to suction out any esophageal contents.

The operator grasps the esophageal wall while the first assistant holds the opposite end. The anvil of a 25mm circular stapler is inserted into the esophagus, and the previously made purse string suture is tied using Ruder's method. The circular stapler is opened inside the abdomen and inserted into the anvil.

The surgeon cuts the nylon tape securing the jejunum and circular stapler, then fires the stapler. After firing, the stapler is opened and carefully withdrawn to prevent tearing the anastomosis. The entry hole is resected using a 60mm linear stapler cartridge.
